g.text: tests. That size makes it a coupling magnet: small
generators/tests in the short term (next release)
and create substantial coordination cost over the
medium term (few releases) as callers evolve.
reduces duplication today but increases the risk
that future API tweaks become high-impact change
events that block parallel work and pressure teams
boundary erosion). Evidence: the changeset shows
generator config bits from the Spring-focused test
module. Tests reaching into core config create a usage
pattern that developers will copy into production
code over the medium term, causing package/layer
boundary drift. Tradeoff: pulling core knobs into
tests gives immediate coverage and convenience but
narrows future freedom to change core configuration
without breaking consumers across language modules;
consequence is that intended layering decisions become
harder to reverse within one-to-two releases.

compose with core classes change, it often
signals a behavioral contract change rather than
a pure refactor. Consequence: downstream language
implementations and template consumers are likely
to need updates within the next release window,
Tradeoff: evolving the core contract can unlock
improved behavior but raises immediate upgrade work
